---
title: "Exploratory Analysis"
author: "Nutchanon Chaiyaratana"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Overview

This page explores the cleaned Lightcast job postings dataset created in the data preparation step. The purpose of this exploratory analysis is to understand the overall size of the dataset, salary availability, remote-work categories, geographic distribution, and industry distribution before moving into the main research analysis.

## 1. Load Cleaned Data

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from IPython.display import display
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("EDAJobMarket") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

df_clean = spark.read.parquet("data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet")
df_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs_clean")

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled

print("Cleaned rows:", df_clean.count())
print("Cleaned columns:", len(df_clean.columns))
```

## 2. Preview Cleaned Dataset

```{python}
#| echo: true

preview_cols = [
    "ID",
    "TITLE_CLEAN",
    "COMPANY_NAME",
    "CITY_NAME",
    "STATE_NAME",
    "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
    "SALARY",
    "SALARY_FROM",
    "SALARY_TO",
    "ANALYSIS_SALARY",
    "POSTED_DATE"
]

available_preview_cols = [c for c in preview_cols if c in df_clean.columns]

preview_df = df_clean.select(available_preview_cols).limit(10).toPandas()

display(style_table(preview_df))
```

## 3. Cleaned Dataset Summary

```{python}
#| echo: true

summary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_job_postings,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ID) AS unique_job_ids,
    COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_NAME) AS unique_companies,
    COUNT(DISTINCT STATE_NAME) AS unique_states,
    COUNT(DISTINCT NAICS_2022_6_NAME) AS unique_industries,
    COUNT(DISTINCT LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME) AS unique_occupations
FROM raw_jobs_clean
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(summary, {
    "total_job_postings": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_job_ids": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_companies": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_states": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_industries": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_occupations": "{:,.0f}"
}))
```

## 4. Salary Availability After Imputation

This section checks salary coverage using `ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED`, which combines observed salary values with qualified industry-median imputed salary values. This variable is used as the main salary variable for later salary-based analysis.

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

salary_availability = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END AS salary_status,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM raw_jobs_clean) * 100, 2) AS job_share
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(salary_availability, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.2f}%"
}))
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

fig_salary = px.bar(
    salary_availability,
    x="salary_status",
    y="job_count",
    text="job_count",
    color="salary_status",
    color_discrete_map={
        "Salary Missing": "#EF553B",
        "Salary Available": "#00CC96"
    },
    title="Salary Availability After Imputation",
    labels={
        "salary_status": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    }
)

fig_salary.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_salary.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=380,
    margin=dict(l=50, r=40, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_salary.show()
```

**Insight:** After industry-median imputation, 39,244 postings have usable salary values and 33,232 postings remain missing salary. This increases the salary sample size compared with the original `ANALYSIS_SALARY` variable, which had 32,394 observed salary values.

## Salary Missingness Bias Check

This section checks whether salary availability still differs across important groups after using the imputed salary variable. This helps identify whether salary-based results may still be affected by missing salary patterns.

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

ai_job_ids = spark.sql("""
SELECT DISTINCT
    ID AS job_id
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE
    LOWER(TITLE_CLEAN) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(TITLE_CLEAN) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(TITLE_CLEAN) LIKE '%ai engineer%'
    OR LOWER(TITLE_CLEAN) LIKE '%ml engineer%'
    OR LOWER(TITLE_CLEAN) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(TITLE_CLEAN) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%large language model%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(BODY) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
    OR LOWER(SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
    OR LOWER(SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME) LIKE '%pytorch%'
    OR LOWER(SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME) LIKE '%tensorflow%'
""")

ai_job_ids.createOrReplaceTempView("ai_job_ids")


salary_missing_by_ai = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT r.ID) AS total_postings,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN r.ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND r.ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN r.ID
    END) AS salary_available,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN r.ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
             OR r.ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
        THEN r.ID
    END) AS salary_missing,
    ROUND(
        COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
            WHEN r.ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
                 OR r.ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
            THEN r.ID
        END) / COUNT(DISTINCT r.ID) * 100,
        2
    ) AS missing_salary_percent
FROM raw_jobs_clean r
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON r.ID = a.job_id
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END
ORDER BY missing_salary_percent DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(salary_missing_by_ai, {
    "total_postings": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_available": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_missing": "{:,.0f}",
    "missing_salary_percent": "{:.2f}%"
}))


salary_missing_by_remote = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    REMOTE_TYPE_NAME AS remote_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ID) AS total_postings,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN ID
    END) AS salary_available,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
             OR ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
        THEN ID
    END) AS salary_missing,
    ROUND(
        COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
            WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
                 OR ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
            THEN ID
        END) / COUNT(DISTINCT ID) * 100,
        2
    ) AS missing_salary_percent
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE REMOTE_TYPE_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY REMOTE_TYPE_NAME
ORDER BY missing_salary_percent DESC
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(salary_missing_by_remote, {
    "total_postings": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_available": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_missing": "{:,.0f}",
    "missing_salary_percent": "{:.2f}%"
}))


salary_missing_by_state = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    STATE_NAME AS state_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ID) AS total_postings,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN ID
    END) AS salary_available,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
             OR ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
        THEN ID
    END) AS salary_missing,
    ROUND(
        COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
            WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
                 OR ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
            THEN ID
        END) / COUNT(DISTINCT ID) * 100,
        2
    ) AS missing_salary_percent
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE STATE_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY STATE_NAME
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT ID) >= 100
ORDER BY missing_salary_percent DESC
LIMIT 20
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(salary_missing_by_state, {
    "total_postings": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_available": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_missing": "{:,.0f}",
    "missing_salary_percent": "{:.2f}%"
}))


salary_missing_by_industry = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    NAICS_2022_6_NAME AS industry,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ID) AS total_postings,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NOT NULL
             AND ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED > 0
        THEN ID
    END) AS salary_available,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
        WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
             OR ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
        THEN ID
    END) AS salary_missing,
    ROUND(
        COUNT(DISTINCT CASE
            WHEN ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED IS NULL
                 OR ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED <= 0
            THEN ID
        END) / COUNT(DISTINCT ID) * 100,
        2
    ) AS missing_salary_percent
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE NAICS_2022_6_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY NAICS_2022_6_NAME
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT ID) >= 100
ORDER BY missing_salary_percent DESC
LIMIT 20
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(salary_missing_by_industry, {
    "total_postings": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_available": "{:,.0f}",
    "salary_missing": "{:,.0f}",
    "missing_salary_percent": "{:.2f}%"
}))
```

**Insight:** Salary missingness remains important even after industry-median imputation. The imputed salary variable improves coverage, but missingness can still vary by AI classification, remote type, state, and industry. Salary-based results should therefore be interpreted with this remaining missingness in mind.

## 5. Remote Type Distribution

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

remote_summary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN REMOTE_TYPE_NAME IS NULL OR REMOTE_TYPE_NAME = '[None]' THEN 'Unknown'
        ELSE REMOTE_TYPE_NAME
    END AS remote_type,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN REMOTE_TYPE_NAME IS NULL OR REMOTE_TYPE_NAME = '[None]' THEN 'Unknown'
        ELSE REMOTE_TYPE_NAME
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

remote_summary["job_share"] = (
    remote_summary["job_count"] / remote_summary["job_count"].sum()
)

display(style_table(remote_summary, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.1%}"
}))
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

fig_remote = px.bar(
    remote_summary,
    x="remote_type",
    y="job_count",
    text="job_count",
    color="remote_type",
    color_discrete_map={
        "Unknown": "#636EFA",
        "Remote": "#00CC96",
        "Hybrid Remote": "#AB63FA",
        "Not Remote": "#FFA15A"
    },
    title="Remote Type Distribution",
    labels={
        "remote_type": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    }
)

fig_remote.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_remote.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=380,
    margin=dict(l=50, r=40, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_remote.show()
```

**Insight:** Remote-work status is not always clearly identified in the dataset, so the `Unknown` category is kept instead of being assumed to be on-site. Later remote-work salary comparisons should filter to clearly labeled categories such as Remote, Hybrid Remote, and Not Remote.

## 6. Top 10 States by Job Postings

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

top_states = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    STATE_NAME AS state_name,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE STATE_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY STATE_NAME
ORDER BY job_count DESC
LIMIT 10
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(top_states, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}"
}))
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

top_states_plot = top_states.copy()
top_states_plot["state_label"] = (
    top_states_plot["state_name"]
    + " ("
    + top_states_plot["job_count"].map(lambda x: f"{x:,.0f}")
    + ")"
)

top_states_plot = top_states_plot.sort_values("job_count", ascending=True)

fig_states = px.bar(
    top_states_plot,
    x="job_count",
    y="state_label",
    color="state_name",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top 10 States by Job Postings",
    labels={
        "state_label": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    },
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set3
)

fig_states.update_traces(
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_states.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=480,
    margin=dict(l=180, r=80, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    xaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_states.show()
```

**Insight:** The top states show where job-posting activity is most concentrated in the dataset. These states are important for later geographic salary comparisons because they provide larger sample sizes than smaller markets.

## 7. Top 10 Industries by Job Postings

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

top_industries = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    NAICS_2022_6_NAME AS industry,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE NAICS_2022_6_NAME IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY NAICS_2022_6_NAME
ORDER BY job_count DESC
LIMIT 10
""").toPandas()

display(style_table(top_industries, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}"
}))
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

top_industries_plot = top_industries.copy()

label_map = {
    "Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services": "Mgmt Consulting",
    "Custom Computer Programming Services": "Computer Programming",
    "Computer Systems Design Services": "Systems Design",
    "Employment Placement Agencies": "Employment Agencies",
    "Direct Health and Medical Insurance Carriers": "Health Insurance",
    "Offices of Certified Public Accountants": "CPA Offices",
    "Other Computer Related Services": "Other Computer Services",
    "Temporary Help Services": "Temporary Help",
    "Unclassified Industry": "Unclassified"
}

top_industries_plot["industry_short"] = top_industries_plot["industry"].replace(label_map)

top_industries_plot["industry_label"] = (
    top_industries_plot["industry_short"]
    + " ("
    + top_industries_plot["job_count"].map(lambda x: f"{x:,.0f}")
    + ")"
)

# ascending=True makes highest value appear at the top in horizontal Plotly charts
top_industries_plot = top_industries_plot.sort_values("job_count", ascending=True)

fig_industries = px.bar(
    top_industries_plot,
    x="job_count",
    y="industry_label",
    text="job_count",
    color="industry_short",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top 10 Industries by Job Postings",
    labels={
        "industry_label": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    },
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set3
)

fig_industries.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_industries.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=520,
    margin=dict(l=260, r=90, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    xaxis=dict(
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)",
        title="Number of Job Postings"
    ),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="",
        automargin=True
    )
)

fig_industries.show()
```

**Insight:** The industry distribution shows which sectors contribute the largest number of job postings. If an unclassified industry category is large, final industry-specific comparisons should either treat it as a data-quality category or exclude it when comparing clearly defined industries.

## EDA Summary Findings

The exploratory analysis shows that the cleaned dataset contains 72,476 job postings and uses `ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED` as the main salary variable for salary-based analysis. After industry-median imputation, 39,244 postings have usable salary values and 33,232 postings still have missing salary information. Salary missingness remains important and should be considered when interpreting salary-based results. The remote-work distribution contains a large unknown category, so later remote comparisons should focus on clearly labeled remote, hybrid, and not-remote postings. The geographic and industry distributions show where job-posting activity is concentrated and help guide later salary, AI, and regional analysis.
